real_questions.text: Walk us through your leverage on our metrics and against the regulatory asset coverage ratio — and the bridge between the two.
What are your leverage targets — clearly and specifically?
As the balance sheet grows and capital levels decline, how does capitalization remain a credit strength?
What is your TCE to tangible managed assets versus peers?
What is your MSR-to-TCE ratio — and how does it look after a 15% MSR haircut?

common_pitfalls.text: Carrying large intangibles into the Moody's capital conversation — TCE/TMA is the metric, and heavy intangibles can score very weak on capital.

strong_answer_markers.text: Commits to a capitalization floor alongside the growth plan.
Knows TCE/TMA versus peers and MSR/TCE with the 15% haircut applied before the meeting.

agency_intel.text: On BDC leverage, agencies take a dual approach: their own leverage metrics first, with the regulatory asset coverage ratio layered on top — similar to how they run their own bank capital evaluation against regulatory expectations. Know both numbers and the bridge.

For consumer/specialty lenders: the capital anchor depends on the model — off-balance-sheet-focused lenders are anchored on a leverage metric; on-balance-sheet lenders on tangible capital as the buffer against losses.

agency_intel: For CCPs and market infrastructure entities: regulatory capital provides a moat that supports higher ratings and strong credit protection — present the default-fund and capital waterfall framework affirmatively, as a credit strength.

agency_intel.text: Moody's evaluates CRE lender leverage on debt-to-equity — viewed as broadly stable across the group but actively monitored.

Moody's non-bank mortgage framework: origination volume, quality concentrations, funding reliability. Capital is TCE to tangible managed assets versus peers; MSR/TCE measures capital quality (higher = weaker) with a 15% haircut applied to MSRs.
